coredata.dc:title: Design of C-dots/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite as an efficient new nanozyme and its application for determination of H2O2 in nanomolar level
coredata.dc:creator: Yousefinejad, Saeed
Rasti, Hamid
Hajebi, Mehdi
Kowsari, Masoud
Sadravi, Shima
Honarasa, Fatemeh
coredata.dc:description: A new nanocomposite was synthesized by using of carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles as precursors. TEM images and spectra of FT-IR, XRD and EDS confirmed the presence of nanocomposite in the solution. The synthesized nanocomposite shows intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. The main advantage of synthesized nanocomposite is its higher peroxidase-like activity rather than both individual C-dots and magnetic nanoparticles. Also, the introduced nanocomposite was used for colorimetric determination of hydrogen peroxide with linear range of 1.0×10−8 M to 1.0×10−3 M and LOD of 1.0×10−9 M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
C-dots
Magnetic nanoparticles
Horseradish peroxidase
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Horseradish peroxidase-like activity of C-dots/iron oxide magnetic nanocomposite
Analytical application for determination of hydrogen peroxide

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of (a) C-dots, (b) bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles and (c) C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposites, (B) XRD pattern of as-prepared C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite and (C) TEM images of (a) Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, (b) as-prepared C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite.
Absorption of solution containing 8.3×10−4 M TMB, 0.1M H2O2 and (a) 140mgL−1 Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, (b) 3.2% (V/V) C-dots and (c) 13mgL−1 C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite at 652nm vs. time.
Comparison of catalytic dynamic parameters (H2O2 as substrate) between C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite and other reported nanozymes.
Colorimetric detection of H2O2 with different nanomaterials as peroxidase nanozymes.

head.ce:title: Design of C-dots/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite as an efficient new nanozyme and its application for determination of H2O2 in nanomolar level

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Health Sciences, Department of Occupational Health Engineering, School of Health, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shiraz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Shiraz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new nanocomposite was synthesized by using of carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles as precursors. TEM images and spectra of FT-IR, XRD and EDS confirmed the presence of nanocomposite in the solution. The synthesized nanocomposite shows intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. The main advantage of synthesized nanocomposite is its higher peroxidase-like activity rather than both individual C-dots and magnetic nanoparticles. Also, the introduced nanocomposite was used for colorimetric determination of hydrogen peroxide with linear range of 1.0×10−8 M to 1.0×10−3 M and LOD of 1.0×10−9 M.

ce:section.ce:para: Design of nanostructures based on function, principle, or even structure of enzymes leads to open a new horizon in biomimetics which is known as enzyme mimicking [1–3]. Serious shortcoming of natural enzymes including low performance, digestion/denaturation in harsh environmental condition, low stability and high cost of purification hinder the use of natural enzymes [3,4]. As a result, nanozyme as a new generation of enzymes has been received a lot of attention [2]. In fact, nanozymes are nanomaterials with artificial enzyme mimicking activities [4]. One of the most applicable classes of nanozymes is magnetic nanoparticles. In 2007, Gao et al. reported the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of magnetic nanoparticles [5]. Since then, several efforts were reported to increase the enzymatic activity of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [6–10].

Hybrid magnetic nanoparticles (magnetic nanocomposites) show the advantages of both constituents properties [11]. Due to the wide advantages of carbon nanoallotropes [12,13], this kind of materials was used in synthesis of magnetic nanocomposites. Carbon based nanomaterials improve the stability of magnetic nanocomposites and expand their applications [14]. So, one of the most attracting constituents for construction of magnetic nanocomposites are carbon based materials [14–16]. In this manner, carbon materials including amorphous carbon [17], carbon nanotubes [18–21], graphene [22] and graphene oxide [23,24] were used for synthesis of magnetic nanocomposites. The horseradish peroxidase-like activity of helical carbon nanotubes [25], carbon nanotube/Fe3O4 [26], graphene oxide/Fe3O4 [27,28] and graphene oxide/carbon nanotube/Fe3O4 [29] nanocomposites were reported, previously. Formation of carbon nanotube/Fe3O4 nanocomplex enhanced the peroxidase-like activity of magnetic nanoparticles [26]. But despite of high peroxidase-like activity of carbon naotube/Fe3O4 nanocomposite, this nanocomposite presents low dispersion in aqueous system. To solve this problem, graphene oxide as a surfactant was employed to create a stable support of graphene/carbon nanotube for magnetic nanoparticles loading [29]. In this way, a new nanocomposite with higher enzymatic activity in aqueous media was proposed.

On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) as newly introduced carbon-based materials emerge unique characteristics such as chemical inertness, low toxicity, good water solubility, remarkable optical properties, simple synthetic route, ease of functionalization and low cost [30,31]. Also, this kind of nanostructures presents intrinsic peroxidase-like activity [32]. It seems that, carbon quantum dots due to their peroxidase-like activity and good water solubility are good choices for construction of a new magnetic nanocomposite. Moreover, there are some reports for synthesis of Fe2O3/C-dots composites as photocatalyst [33,34]. But, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report for synthesis of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite as a nanozyme.

Colorimetry as an analytical technique was used for determination of the concentration of colored compounds in a solution. In comparison with other analytical techniques, the colorimetric assay is simple and rapid, and the detection of analytes can also be observed by the naked eye [3]. Mostly, colorimetric determination of hydrogen peroxide is based on the use of horseradish peroxidase in an enzymatic reaction [35]. But due to the shortcoming of natural enzymes, artificial enzymes and later nanozymes as alternatives has been employed. Besides the several distinct advantages such as low cost, easy of mass production, robustness to harsh environments, high stability, long term storage and tunable activity shared with other artificial enzymes, nanozymes are unique due to their nanoscale sizes. Also, nanoscale materials have a sufficiently large area to conjugate with other species for biorecognition [2].

In this work, a new heterogeneous nanocomposite was designed based on magnetic nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots. After characterization of synthesized nanocomposite, the enzymatic activity of the introduced nanohybrid was investigated. This heterogeneous hybrid can be introduced as synergic material because of well beyond properties and high performance of a simple mixture of its components. Finally as an analytical application, the as-prepared nanocomposite was used for determination of hydrogen peroxide by using of colorimetric method.
Carbon soot was collected from a burning candle. All other chemical with analytical grade were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Firstly, C-dots were synthesized based on previously reported procedure [36,37]. Briefly, carbon soot (25mg) was mixed with nitric acid (5M). Then the solution was refluxed and the black solution was cooled and centrifuged to separate out unreacted carbon soot. The light brownish-yellow supernatant was used for further study. Also, magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles were synthesized by using partial reduction method in alkali media [26]. In a nutshell, solution of sodium sulfite (0.16molL−1) was added slowly into the solution of FeCl3 (0.05molL−1). Then, ammonia was rapidly injected and reaction was kept at 60°C for 30min. The precipitated powder was isolated after 2 more hours by using of a permanent magnete (1.4T). The magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles were dispersed in 0.01molL−1 hydrochloric acid solution.

To prepare nanocomposite, the stock solution of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and C-dots were mixed together by ratio of 1:4 in acidic media (pH 2). Then after 30min, the synthesized nanocomposite was magnetically separated and washed twice with pure water. The as-prepared nanocomposite was dispersed in distillated water with a loading of 1.0gL−1.

XRD patterns were performed by D8 ADVANCE type (BRUKER-Germany) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ=0.1542nm). Images of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were obtained by Zeiss, EM10C (accelerating voltage of 80kV). FT-IR spectra (Transmission mode) were obtained by PerkinElmer FT-IR Spectrum RX I. A resolution of 1cm−1 was used to scan a spectral range between 4000 and 400cm−1. UV–vis spectra were recorded using Hach DR 5000 spectrophotometer with spectral resolution of 1nm at room temperature. The pH measurements were carried out by using a Metrohm pH meter (model 780).

To compare the enzyme-like activity of C-dots, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite, working solutions contain 0.1M H2O2 and 8.3×10−4 M 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in presence of 140mgL−1 Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, 3.2% (V/V) C-dots or 13mgL−1 C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite were prepared freshly. Then, absorption values at 652nm were collected during 300s.
The sensing solution for H2O2 determination contains 8.3×10−4 M TMB, 15mgL−1 C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite and different concentration of H2O2. Then UV–vis spectra were recorded after 30min at 25°C.

To test the batch-to-batch reproducibility of the as-prepared nanocomposite, the nanocomposite was synthesized three times from different batch of both precursors. Then, UV–vis spectra of solutions contain 0.005M H2O2, 8.3×10−4 M TMB and 15mgL−1 of each nanocomposite were recorded after 30min.

To synthesize C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite, firstly both C-dots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were prepared, separately. For C-dots, a maximum absorption peak at ∼300nm of UV–vis spectrum was observed (Fig. S1) which is attributed to the n–π* and π−π* transitions of the CO and CC bands, respectively [38]. Then, C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposites were prepared simply by mixing the solutions of C-dots and Fe3O4 in ratio of 4:1. pH of mixed solution should be adjusted in acidic media (pH 2.0) to have more active nanozyme. After 30min, the interaction of both nanostructures is completed. Then, the prepared nanocomposite was washed and separate with magnet several times to remove the excess amount of C-dots. FT-IR spectra of C-dots, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles and C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposites were depicted at Fig. 1 A. FT-IR spectrum of C-dots presents the peaks at 1631 and 1416cm−1 which are attributed to CO stretching vibrational band [39]. Also, the characteristic peak of OH was observed at 3422cm−1. For Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles, vibrational band of Fe-O was observed at 584cm−1. Also, the broad peak at ∼3400cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching band. Obviously, after interaction of C-dots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles and synthesis of C-dots/Fe3O4, the characteristic peak of Fe-O at the nanocomposite was observed at 568cm−1 which shows the presence of iron oxide in nanocomposite structure. Also, the characteristic peak of Fe-O was shifted compared to the corresponding peak of bare magnetic nanoparticles which is due to the interaction of iron oxide and C-dots [22]. Additionally, the characteristic peaks of CO at 1400 and 1600cm−1 present at nanocomposite FT-IR spectrum. So, the presence of C-dots, iron oxide and interaction of two components were confirmed by FT-IR spectra.

Fig. 1B represents the XRD pattern of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite. The corresponding pattern of bare Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles can be seen at this pattern, i.e., peaks at 30.1°, 35.7°, 43.1°, 53.5°, 56.9°, 62.6° and 74.2° can be observed which are corresponding to the Miller indices of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), and (533), respectively. These indices confirm the spinel structure of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [40]. Another peak with low intensity can be observed at ∼26° which is due to the presence of C-dots in the nanocomposite. The characteristic peak of carbon at ∼26 is too weak which is difficult to distinguish from background noise. This is due to the small quantity of C-dots in the nanocomposite [41,42]. Also, Fig.S2 displays X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) patterns of corresponding nanocomposite. Clearly, elements of C, Fe and O present in the synthesized nanocomposite.

Morphology of the as-prepared nanocomposite was characterized by transmission electron microscope. Fig. 1C depicted the transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of bare Fe3O4 and corresponding nanocomposite. As it is clear, Fe3O4 nanoparticles are semi-spherical with size ranging about 10nm. Also, in case of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the TEM image shows the presence of C-dots in the nanocomposite. In this case, two sets of nanostructures, i.e. Fe3O4 nanoparticles and C-dots with different sizes can be distinguished. C-dots with average size of ∼2nm are smaller than Fe3O4 nanoparticles (∼10nm). Obviously, both constituents preserve their nano dimensions.

It seems that in acidic media, water soluble Fe3O4 nanoparticles with OH terminated surface present active surface to interact with OH and CO2H groups on the C-dots. This process is the same procedure of interaction of carbon nanotube and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Horseradish peroxidase-like activity of C-dots/iron oxide magnetic nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles individually possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and good water solubility. Besides, magnetic nanoparticles have been attracted due to the magnetism property. Considering such significant properties, the combination of both components may be regarded as an ideal strategy to construct stable and efficient nanozyme with peroxidase-like activity. So, colorimetric tests were performed to calculate peroxidase-like activity of the synthesized nanocomposite. In this manner, catalytic oxidation of TMB as a substrate in the presence of H2O2 was investigated. Enzymatic activities of all three nanostructures (i.e., C-dots, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles and C-dots/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite) within 300s were explored and results are depicted in Fig. 2 . All three nanostructures, i.e., C-dots, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and C-dots/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite could catalyze oxidation reaction of TMB in the presence of H2O2 [5,32]. Based on previous report, the catalytic mechanism of C-dots probably originates from an increase in the electron density and mobility in the C-dots because of electron transfer from lone-pair electrons in TMB amino groups to the C-dots. This would result in acceleration of electron transfer from the C-dots to H2O2, and the reaction rate of TMB oxidation by H2O2 would increased [32]. So the corresponding curve reaches to the plateau earlier rather than corresponding curves of both other nanostructures . Also, in case of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the presence of ferrous and ferric ions in the Fe3O4 nanoparticles is likely to be the key to their catalysis [5]. But in case of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite, presence of ferrous and ferric ions in addition to the small quantity of C-dots changed the time of reaching to the plateau. Also, variations in absorbance for Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles and C-dots/Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposite are linear while the non-linear slope can be observed for C-dots curve. The lowest slope can be observed for Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles which indicate the lower activity of the Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles rather than both other nanostructures. In case of C-dots, higher absorbance variations in earlier times can be observed but at time larger than 200s, the absorbance reaches to a steady state. It seems that the maximum activity of C-dots was obtained at shorter times rather than both other nanozymes. Also, at larger times, absorbance of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite in the presence of TMB and H2O2 at 652nm is higher than the corresponding curves of both individual nanoparticles. In fact, the as-prepared nanocomposite produces greater color change. So, C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite shows faster kinetics rather than individual nanoparticles.

The enzymatic activity of C-dots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles like horseradish peroxidase depend on pH, H2O2 and enzyme/nanozyme concentration. So, dependency on these parameters was also tested for C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite (Fig. S3). Clearly, the maximum relative activities were observed at pH value of 4.0, 0.2M H2O2 and 15mgL−1 of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite. Optimum pH value of 4.0 is near to the optimum pH values of individual C-dots, Fe3O4 and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [5,32]. Also in case of H2O2 concentration, the higher activity was observed at lower H2O2 concentration (i.e., 0.2M) rather than corresponding values of both individual C-dots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (i.e., 0.3M and 0.5M, respectively) [5,32].

To evaluate the mechanism of enzyme mimetic of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite, kinetic study was performed. The kinetic data were obtained by changing H2O2 concentration as substrate while fixing of TMB concentration. In this manner, typical Michaelis–Menten curves were observed. The Michaelis-Menten constant was calculated based on the Lineweaver-Burk plot: 1/v =Km/Vmax·C+1/Vmax, where v is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, and C is the concentration of hydrogen peroxide. Km is an inverse measure of the affinity of binding between the enzyme and its substrate. In this manner, parameters of maximum reaction rate (Vmax) and apparent Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) were obtained as 1.4×10−7 Ms−1 and 3.5mM, respectively. Table 1 summarized catalytic dynamic parameters of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite and some other reported carbonecous nanozymes. In case of C-dots/Fe3O4, Km value is lower than the corresponding values of C-dots [32] and Fe3O4 [5] nanozymes and is near to the Km value of HRP natural enzyme [5] and nanozyme of GO-COOH [43]. This fact verified higher maximum activity of C-dots/Fe3O4 in lower H2O2 concentration rather than both C-dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles [5,32]. Also, Vmax of C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite is higher than the corresponding values of HRP [5], Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles [5], GO-COOH [43], GO-Fe3O4 [27] and GCNT-Fe3O4 [29] nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application for determination of hydrogen peroxide
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared nanocomposite was used for colorimetric determination of H2O2. The variation in absorbance at 652nm is an indication of H2O2 concentration. So, calibration curve for H2O2 was evaluated in the presence of 8.3×10−4 M TMB and 15mgL−1 C-dots/Fe3O4 nanozyme (Fig. 3 ). Such as the previous report, the absorbance value of 750nm was subtracted from the absorbance value of 652nm to obtain the real peak intensity [44]. In this manner, the regression line (R2 =0.995) was obtained with the linear range of 1.0×10−8 M to 1.0×10−3 M. Also, experimental limit of detection (LOD) for hydrogen peroxide as the lowest quantity of hydrogen peroxide that can be distinguished from the blank was obtained as low as 1.0×10−9 M. The analytical figures for determination of H2O2 were compared with other reported carbon nanozyme (Table 2 ). Obviously, linear range and LOD of the as-prepared Fe3O4/C-dots are better than the corresponding values of Fe3O4 [45], C-dots [32], GO-Fe3O4 [27] and GCNT-Fe3O4 [29] nanozymes.

Also, the batch-to-batch reproducibility of the C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite synthesis was examined by comparison the enzyme-like activity of three nanocomposites with different batch of synthesis. So, absorption of three different test solutions contains 5mM H2O2, 8.3×10−4 M TMB and 15mgL−1 of each nanocomposite was obtained at 652nm. In this manner, RSD of 11.0% was obtained which shows acceptable reproducibility in the synthesis procedure.

C-dots and magnetic nanoparticles were implemented to synthesize a new C-dots/Fe3O4 nanocomposite. The synthesis procedure is easy and reproducibility for synthesis is acceptable with RSD of 11.0%. Presence of nanocomposite was confirmed by XRD, FT-IR, EDS and TEM. The introduced nanocomposite shows intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and can make a color change in TMB solution. This nanocomposite shows higher activity rather than HRP, C-dots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles. The newly synthesize

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the support of this work by Islamic Azad University, Shiraz Branch (Shiraz, Iran) and Iran National Science Foundation (INSF), Grant #94811186.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.145.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Decorating carbon nanotubes with metal or semiconductor nanoparticles

Superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles–carbon nitride nanotube hybrids for highly efficient peroxidase mimetic catalysts
Superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanocrystals@graphene composites for energy storage devices
One step synthesis of graphene oxide-magnetic nanoparticle composite
Superparamagnetic graphene oxide-Fe3O4 nanoparticles hybrid for controlled targeted drug carriers
Helical carbon nanotubes: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application for biocatalysis and biosensing
Design of a carbon nanotube/magnetic nanoparticle-based peroxidase-like nanocomplex and its application for highly efficient catalytic oxidation of phenols
Graphene oxide–Fe3O4 magnetic nanocomposites with peroxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of glucose
Talanta Enhanced nonenzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensing with reduced graphene oxide/ferroferric oxide nanocomposites
Recyclable enzyme mimic of cubic Fe3O4 nanoparticles loaded on graphene oxide-dispersed carbon nanotubes with enhanced peroxidase-like catalysis and electrocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots and their applications

Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Saeed Yousefinejad received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry in 2012 from Chemistry Department at Shiraz University (Shiraz, Iran). After two post-doctoral fellowships in Protein and Bioanalytical Chemistry at University of Tehran and Shiraz University respectively (2012–2016), he has jointed to Shiraz University of Medical Sciences (SUMS) since 2016 as Assistant Professor of Analytical Chemistry. His main current research interests include application of spectroscopy, chromatographic methods, extraction and chemometrics in chemical analysis/bioanalysis of occupational and environmental samples.

Hamid Rasti, Mehdi Hajebi, Masoud Kowsari and Shima Sadravi were the MSc students of Analytical Chemistry (2014–2016) at Department of Chemistry, Islamic Azad University, Shiraz, Iran with research fields on the synthesis, modification and characterization of magnetic nanoparticles and their application in chemical/biochemical sensors under supervision of Dr. F. Honarasa.

Dr. Fatemeh Honarasa was born in 1982. She received her PhD in 2012 in Analytical Chemistry at Shiraz University (Iran). She is now Assistant Professor of Analytical Chemistry in Islamic Azad University, Shiraz, Iran. Currently she is head of a research group in the field of sensors and bio/nanosensors. Her main interest is the development of novel nano-materials for electrochemical, optical and biomedical applications.
